item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must say that the story is very difficult to piece to together entirely but still very interesting

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty amusing article. +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It reminds me of TMBG, in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been interested in VNOs for a while, particularly the fact that humans (apparently) don't have them. Therefore I was predisposed to favor this. It did not disappoint.

The ambiguity - was the voice on the phone an unreliable narrator? - really helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
telemarketing skips

A quick search of the wiki reveals only one mainsite article that explicitly mentions telemarketing. There are other articles that involve phone calls, but none that specifically revolve around people trying to sell stuff over the phone. I'm not sure where this fatigue is coming from.

even kidnapping the person making the calls is an old move.

The last addendum serves to establish that this isn't necessarily true, and while this John Ochieng is never found, it isn't ever established that this is even a real person either. Thomas Holloway alludes to this ambiguity above quite aptly. Yeah, they might have kidnapped someone, although it's equally possible that it's just a ploy to sell more VNOs - and it evidently worked. It's about taking expectations and using them to achieve the sole driving goal of whoever is behind this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

The imagery with VNOs was quite unsettling, but I'm dead sick of telemarketing skips. Every beat of this story is straight off the trope: even kidnapping the person making the calls is an old move.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing that really got me was

A lack of consistency in the behaviour of SCP-3206-A1 in these cases casts some doubt on the authenticity of information garnered from the entity through Call BN0358.

So was the guy really kidnapped or was it a sales tactic tailored to the Foundation?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My god, this is thoroughly chilling. Usually weird marketing skips are comedic, but not only do they have these people at their mercy via threatening their families, it sounds like they just suck the nose organ right out of the horse once you accept it. D: I, for one, am unsettled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lord only nose what's going on here, but something smells fishy and I don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking this was kind of 'meh no-vote', up until the actual phone-call transcript, which was sufficiently disturbing and/or funny and/or terrifying to make it a solid +1.

(The technical polish on the article is also quite excellent. One tiny nit: I'd add a footnote for your reference to Equus ferus caballus to mention that this is basically just a horse, just to avoid any confusion)

item.title: Noice?
item.content:encoded:
You know, I didn't really mind this. Not much I can do to articulate it better, I just thought this was pretty weird.

I'd like to see a bit more about their attempts to track down these people. But apart from this, this was a +1 from me.

item.title: Proposition for you
item.content:encoded:
Because phone calls and novel ways of perceiving the world can be unsettling, right?

Many thanks to Chubert and Dr Knowledge for reading and critiquing in the forums.

Below image attribution: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flehmen_response#/media/File:FlehmenResponseHorse.jpg, public domain.

have a flehmen response
away
